title: Electron momentum densities from QSGW and $G^0W^0$: Revealing the role of many-body effects within the reduced density matrix
authors: A. D. N. James, J. A. Gould, T. M. Mason, J. Jackson, S. B. Dugdale
abstract: The ground-state many-body electron momentum density, which can be probed by x-ray Compton scattering, holds insights into the electronic structure of materials. Comparisons between the measured so-called Compton profiles and the theoretical ones are invaluable in assessing the successes and failures of the methodology used to generate the theoretical ground-state electronic structure. Here, we present calculations of the Compton profiles of Li, Si, Cr, and Ni using the state-of-the-art QSGW method within the Questaal package compared with density functional theory (DFT), one-shot $GW$ ($G^0W^0$) predictions and with experiment. This comparison reveals significant differences between the QSGW and $G^0W^0$ methods which we attribute to the distinction between the single particle density provided by the QSGW method and the many-body density that we construct from the $G^0W^0$ theory; although in general the QSGW description of the electronic structure is superior to that of $G^0W^0$, we find the use of the many-body reduced density matrix is key to improving the agreement of the Compton profile with experiment.
summary: This work investigates how well theoretical methods predict the electron momentum density by comparing calculated Compton profiles against experimental measurements for several elements. The authors find that while advanced methods like QSGW improve upon DFT, the inclusion of the full many-body reduced density matrix, as captured by $G^0W^0$, is essential for achieving the best agreement with experiment. This highlights the critical role of correlation effects in determining the true electronic structure.
main_problem: Assessing the accuracy of theoretical methods in describing the ground-state many-body electron momentum density (EMD) by comparing predictions to experimental Compton scattering data.
main_result: The use of the many-body reduced density matrix, particularly within the $G^0W^0$ framework, significantly improves agreement with experimental Compton profiles compared to single-particle approximations like DFT or QSGW.
method: Theoretical calculations employ advanced many-body perturbation theory methods, specifically QSGW and $G^0W^0$, to compute the EMD, which is then used to predict the Compton profile.
model_or_system: The study applies these methods to several elemental materials, including Li, Si, Cr, and Ni, to test the robustness of the theoretical framework across different electronic structures.
key_observables: Compton profile ($J(p_z)$), Electron Momentum Density ($ho(\mathbf{p})$), and the auto-correlation function ($B(r)$).
important_parameters: The comparison hinges on the distinction between single-particle descriptions (DFT, QSGW) and those incorporating full many-body occupation effects ($G^0W^0$).
main_assumptions: The primary approximations involve the use of the $G^0W^0$ self-energy approximation and the reliance on the reduced density matrix formalism to capture correlation effects.
figures_summary: Figures compare experimental Compton profiles and directional differences for Li, Si, Cr, and Ni against predictions from DFT, QSGW, and $G^0W^0$ across various high-symmetry directions.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares results for multiple materials (Li, Si, Cr, Ni), first establishing the theoretical framework (DFT vs. GW methods) and then demonstrating, through specific material examples, that incorporating the many-body reduced density matrix is crucial for matching experimental observables.
why_interesting: The focus on probing the many-body nature of the ground state via Compton scattering provides a unique, highly sensitive diagnostic tool to validate complex correlated electron theories beyond standard band structure calculations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Left column: Comparison of three high-symmetry direction ([001], [011], [111]) experimental Li Compton profiles (CPs) from Sakurai et al. [33] (dots) with our Elk (LDA), Elk G0W 0, Questaal DFT, and Questaal QSGW CPs. To avoid clutter, the error bars are shown for every fifth data point and are estimated from the relative percentage error reported at pz = 0 of the Sch¨ulke et al. [34] and only serve as an indication of the scale of the error. The insets show the differences between the theory and the experimental data (without error bars) along each high symmetry direction. Right column: The theoretical directional differences of the high symmetry directions ([001], [011], [111])...

FIG. 2. Left column: Comparison of three high-symmetry direction ([001], [011], [111]) experimental Si Compton profiles (CPs) from Erba et al. [10] (dots) with our Elk (LDA), Elk G0W 0, Questaal DFT (LDA) and QSGW CPs. The insets highlight the difference between the theoretical and experimental (without error bars) CPs. We note that the experimental profiles were normalised to the number of electrons the profiles represent up to 10 a.u.. Right column: The theoretical and experimental directional differences of the high symmetry directions ([001], [011], [111]) from the left column. We show the experimental error bars for every fifth data point. We convolute our calculations with a Gaussian...

FIG. 3. Left column: The 1D slice of the theoretical and experimental auto-correlation functions B(rz) of Si along the three high-symmetry directions ([001], [011], [111]). These were obtained by Fourier transforming the experimental and convoluted theoretical Compton profiles in Fig. 2. The inset zooms into B(rz) to highlight it at the lattice vector along each high symmetry. Right column: the auto-correlation function anisotropies between the different high symmetry directions. FIG. 4. (a) The single-particle non-magnetic Cr LDA and Questaal QSGW band structures along the same path as presented in Ref. [67]. The corresponding Fermi surface of Cr as described by the (b) LDA and (c) QSGW. The 2D (once projected) occupation densities n(kx, ky) along the [110] high symmetry direction from (c) LDA, (d) Elk G0W 0, (e) Questaal QSGW and (e) the reconstruction from Dugdale et al. [12, 68]. These theoretical n(kx, ky) are non-magnetic. We note that the presented LDA plots are from Questaal but Elk gives consistent plots for its LDA results. FIG. 5. Left column: Comparison of three high-symmetry direction ([001], [011], [111]) experimental Cr Compton profiles (CPs) from Dugdale et al. [12, 68] (dots with estimated error bars) with our non-magnetic Questaal LDA, Elk LDA, Elk G0W 0 and QSGW CPs. The insets highlight the difference between the theoretical and experimental (without error bars) CPs. Right column: The theoretical and experimental directional differences of the high symmetry directions ([001], [011], [111]) from the left column. The experimental error has been plotted for every fifth data point. We convolute our calculations with an experimental resolution function (0.18 a.u. FWHM).
